site.name: Free Strong Password
site.description: Free password security guides for beginners and families

posts.title: What Makes a Password Strong? A Simple Guide for Everyone
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: No technical knowledge needed. We explain in plain English exactly what makes a password difficult to guess — and how to create one in under a minute.
posts.content:
You don't need to be a tech expert to understand passwords. Think of a password like the key to your front door — the more unique and complex it is, the harder it is for someone to copy it.

The three things that make a password strong

Every strong password has three qualities: length (longer is better), variety (mix letters, numbers, symbols), and uniqueness (never reuse a password on two different sites).

Length matters most

A 16-character password is exponentially harder to guess than an 8-character one. Even a simple password becomes very hard to crack if it's long enough. Think of it this way: each extra character multiplies the difficulty by hundreds.

Easy to remember, hard to guess

The best passwords are ones you didn't make up yourself — ones generated randomly by a trusted tool and then stored in a password manager. Our free generator does exactly this, in seconds.

posts.title: Why You Need a Different Password for Every Website
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: If you use the same password everywhere and one site gets hacked, every account you own is at risk. Here's why — and how to fix it easily.
posts.content:
Imagine using the same key for your house, your car, your office, and your bank. If someone copied that key once, they'd have access to everything. That's exactly what happens when you reuse passwords online.

What is credential stuffing?

When a website gets hacked, your username and password can end up in a list sold to criminals. These criminals then try your login details automatically on hundreds of other websites — banking, email, shopping. This is called credential stuffing, and it happens millions of times every day.

The easy fix

Use our free password generator to create a unique password for every site, then store them all in a free password manager like Bitwarden. You only need to remember one master password — the manager handles the rest.

posts.title: The Best Free Password Managers for Beginners in 2026
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: A password manager remembers all your passwords so you don't have to. We've picked the best beginner-friendly options — some completely free.
posts.content:
A password manager is a secure app that stores all your passwords in one safe place. You create one strong master password, and the manager handles everything else — remembering, filling in, and generating new passwords for you.

Our top picks for beginners

Bitwarden (Free) — Our top recommendation for beginners. Completely free, open-source, and very easy to use. Works on phones, tablets, and computers.

NordPass (Free tier available) — A clean, simple interface perfect for beginners. Excellent autofill on mobile.

Apple Keychain — If you use Apple devices exclusively, the built-in Keychain is free and seamlessly integrated.

posts.title: Two-Factor Authentication Made Simple: A Step-by-Step Guide
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: Two-factor authentication sounds technical but it's actually very simple — and it makes your accounts nearly impossible to hack even if someone knows your password.
posts.content:
Two-factor authentication (often called 2FA or two-step verification) adds a second check when you log in — like a text message code or a notification on your phone. Even if someone steals your password, they still can't get in without this second step.

How to turn it on

Most major websites — including Gmail, Facebook, Amazon, and your bank — offer 2FA in their settings. Look for 'Security', 'Privacy', or 'Account settings' and look for 'Two-step verification' or 'Two-factor authentication'. Enable it and follow the steps.

posts.title: What To Do If You Think You've Been Hacked (Step-by-Step)
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: Don't panic. Follow these steps in order and you'll be able to regain control of your accounts and stop the damage quickly.
posts.content:
If you think someone has access to your account, act quickly but calmly. Follow these steps in order.

Step 1: Change your password immediately

Go to the website or app and find the 'Change password' option (usually in Account or Security settings). Use our free generator to create a new strong password right now.

Step 2: Log out all other devices

Most websites have a 'Sign out everywhere' or 'End all sessions' option. Use this to kick out anyone who may have got in.

Step 3: Enable two-factor authentication

Once your password is changed, turn on two-factor authentication so this can't happen again.

Step 4: Check for damage

Look through your emails and account history for anything you didn't do — purchases, messages sent, settings changed. Report anything suspicious to the website's support team.

posts.title: 7 Common Password Mistakes That Put You at Risk (And How to Fix Them)
posts.author: Sarah Mitchell
posts.excerpt: Most people make the same handful of password mistakes. Find out if you're making them — and how to fix each one in minutes.
posts.content:
Most account hacks aren't because of sophisticated attacks — they're because of simple, avoidable password mistakes. Here are the seven most common ones:

  1. Using your name, birthday, or pet's name — hackers try these first.
  2. Using 'password', '123456', or 'qwerty' — these appear in every hacker's list.
  3. Using the same password on multiple sites — one breach exposes everything.
  4. Making passwords too short — under 12 characters is risky in 2026.
  5. Storing passwords in a text file or sticky note — use a password manager instead.
  6. Sharing passwords via email or text — these can be intercepted.
  7. Never changing compromised passwords — check haveibeenpwned.com regularly.
